headline: Qwen3.7 Full Release: Alibaba's Agent-Optimised Frontier Model
summary: Alibaba released Qwen3.7-Max as a proprietary agent-first model, posting top scores on Terminal-Bench 2.0-Terminus, SWE-Pro, SciCode, and MCP-Mark. It performs consistently across Claude Code, OpenClaw, Qwen Code, and custom agent harnesses, targeting developers who need a capable model across diverse tooling environments.
foryou: Top scores on MCP-Mark and SWE-Pro make this the strongest challenger to Claude Sonnet for tool-heavy agentic coding — run a head-to-head on your benchmark suite before your next model contract renewal.

headline: Anthropic and Microsoft in Talks for Maia AI Chip Supply Deal
summary: Microsoft is in discussions to supply its Maia 200 AI accelerator chips to Anthropic, which is facing compute constraints despite existing partnerships with Amazon and Google. A $5 billion Microsoft investment reportedly underpins the deal. Maia's 30% improved performance over comparable alternatives makes it attractive for Anthropic's inference workloads.

headline: Cursor Hits $3 Billion Annual Revenue Run Rate
summary: Cursor's annualised revenue reached $3 billion in late April 2026, with over 3,000 enterprise customers paying at least $100,000 each annually. The growth makes Cursor one of the fastest-growing software startups ever. SpaceX holds rights to acquire the company as part of a previously disclosed strategic arrangement.

headline: OpenAI Reports $5.7 Billion in Q1 2026 Revenue
summary: OpenAI's Q1 2026 revenue came in at $5.7 billion, surpassing Anthropic's Q1 figure. Strong enterprise ChatGPT adoption and API revenue from third-party applications drove the result. The number was reported ahead of the company's anticipated IPO process.

headline: Microsoft Cancels Claude Code Enterprise Licenses, Moves Developers to Copilot CLI
summary: Microsoft has cancelled its enterprise Claude Code licences and is redirecting internal developers to GitHub Copilot CLI. The move is widely interpreted as cost-driven — Microsoft pays Anthropic for Claude access while also investing $5 billion in the company — and signals that Copilot CLI is being positioned as the Microsoft-endorsed coding agent.

headline: Manus Raising $1 Billion to Buy Back Its Business From Meta After Chinese Regulator Veto
summary: China's regulators ordered Manus to unwind its acquisition by Meta, forcing the founders to raise capital to buy back the operation. They are in active fundraising discussions and may contribute personal funds. The case is an unusual example of a post-close acquisition reversal driven by geopolitical regulation.

headline: AI-Written Short Story Wins Granta Literary Prize, Raises Detection Questions
summary: A short story by Jamir Nazir called 'The Serpent in the Grove', published in Granta, won a literary prize before AI-detection software flagged it as likely machine-generated. The judges called the prose 'evocative' but the pattern of imagery — lists of three, unusual metaphors — matched known LLM writing signatures. The incident reignited debate about AI detection reliability.

headline: Apple Plans to Let iOS 27 Users Choose Between Gemini, Claude, and ChatGPT
summary: Apple is building an 'Extensions' framework for iOS 27 that will allow users to swap the underlying AI model powering Siri and Apple Intelligence tasks. Internal testing with Google and Anthropic models is underway. The feature is expected to be previewed at WWDC 2026 and ship in fall.

headline: Lessons Learned From Building Cloud Agents at Scale — Cursor's Playbook
summary: Cursor's engineering team shared six hard-won lessons from building cloud-based coding agents, including: use durable execution runtimes, isolate each agent run in its own dev environment, design for self-healing infrastructure, and separate agent state from conversation state cleanly. The post is unusually candid about failure modes in production.
foryou: The separation of agent state from conversation state is the single insight most teams get wrong — it prevents runaway context accumulation and makes agent runs resumable, both of which you will need at scale.

headline: AI's Falling Prices Are a Software Story, Not a Hardware One
summary: An analysis argues that the dramatic decline in AI inference costs is driven primarily by algorithmic improvements — better quantisation, attention optimisations, and speculative decoding — rather than chip improvements. This means the deflationary trend will continue regardless of whether hardware advances stall.
foryou: If software is driving cost declines, then frontier model prices will keep falling even if chip shortages persist — a direct argument for delaying any long-term compute commitments and staying on pay-as-you-go inference.
